name: Benzodiazepine Addiction
dsm5Name: Sedative, Hypnotic, or Anxiolytic Use Disorder
overview: Benzodiazepines — including alprazolam (Xanax), clonazepam (Klonopin), lorazepam (Ativan), and diazepam (Valium) — are prescribed for anxiety, insomnia, and seizure disorders. Long-term or high-dose use can produce physiological dependence, tolerance, and a substance use disorder. Benzodiazepine withdrawal is potentially dangerous and, like alcohol, can cause seizures — making medically supervised taper the clinical standard.
signs: Using benzodiazepines in larger amounts or for longer than prescribed
Doctor shopping or obtaining prescriptions from multiple providers
Running out of medication early, requesting early refills
Combining benzodiazepines with alcohol or opioids
Anxiety or panic that only medication "fixes"
Withdrawal symptoms (anxiety, insomnia, tremor) when a dose is missed

withdrawalTimeline.window: 6–24 hours (short-acting, e.g., Xanax) / 1–4 days (long-acting, e.g., Valium)

symptoms: Acute physical symptoms slowly subside on structured taper; sleep and mood remain disrupted
Protracted withdrawal (PAWS) in a subset of patients: persistent anxiety

treatmentApproach.title: Medically supervised taper
treatmentApproach.detail: Abrupt cessation of benzodiazepines can cause seizures and is dangerous. Gradual taper under clinical supervision — often cross-tapering to a long-acting benzodiazepine — is the standard of care.

treatmentApproach.title: Residential treatment
treatmentApproach.detail: Residential care supports patients through extended tapers and provides structured treatment of the anxiety or trauma that often underlies benzodiazepine use.

treatmentApproach.title: Anxiety and trauma-focused therapy
treatmentApproach.detail: CBT, DBT, and trauma-focused therapy address the conditions for which benzodiazepines were originally prescribed — preventing relapse to medication after taper.

treatmentApproach.title: Step-down care
treatmentApproach.detail: PHP, IOP, and outpatient maintain clinical contact through the extended post-taper window when protracted symptoms may still emerge.

localContext: Benzodiazepine prescribing rates in Kentucky remain above the national average, and benzodiazepines are frequently present in polysubstance overdoses alongside opioids. Safe, medically supervised taper is essential to prevent withdrawal-related seizures.
medicaidCoverageSummary: All six Kentucky Medicaid MCOs cover medically supervised benzodiazepine taper in detox, residential, PHP, IOP, and outpatient settings. Because tapers can extend for weeks to months, step-down coverage and coordinated outpatient care are especially important.

faqs.q: Is it safe to stop benzodiazepines cold turkey?
faqs.a: No. Abrupt discontinuation of benzodiazepines can cause seizures and is potentially life-threatening, particularly after long-term or high-dose use. Always work with a clinician on a structured taper.

faqs.q: How long does a benzodiazepine taper take?
faqs.a: Tapers are individualized based on dose, duration of use, and patient response. Typical medically supervised tapers range from several weeks to several months; some patients with long-term or high-dose use require slower tapers.

faqs.q: Does Kentucky Medicaid cover benzodiazepine detox?
faqs.a: Yes. All six Kentucky Medicaid MCOs cover medically supervised benzodiazepine taper across the ASAM continuum when medically necessary.
